sections.title: Fats
sections.narration: 54g of fat. saturated fat, omega3, and polyunsaturated fat are doing most of the work. for nuts, fat quality has to justify the calorie density.
sections.subtitleText: 54g of fat. saturated fat, omega3, and polyunsaturated fat are doing most of the work. for nuts, fat quality has to justify the calorie density.

sections.title: Carbs
sections.narration: 20g of carbs. fibre is doing a lot of the work here. for a nut, the score starts with fat chemistry and then asks what else it brings.

sections.title: Proteins
sections.narration: 17g of protein. bioavailability and amino acid quality are both strong. protein is support here, not the whole argument.
sections.subtitleText: 17g of protein. bioavailability and amino acid quality are both strong. protein is support here, not the whole argument.

sections.title: Vitamins
sections.narration: vitamin e at 20% daily value stands out most. that is only mild support overall.

sections.title: Minerals
sections.narration: magnesium at 32% daily value, zinc at 18% daily value, iron at 18% daily value stand out most. useful, but still not a major mineral edge.
sections.subtitleText: magnesium at 32% daily value, zinc at 18% daily value, iron at 18% daily value stand out most. useful, but still not a major mineral edge.

sections.title: Pros
sections.narration: on the plus side: portable, practical snack food because nuts score real points in life because they are easy to use. fat and texture help satiety because they can hold up better than many snack foods. still a recognisable whole-food option because that keeps them cleaner than most processed snacks.

sections.title: Cons
sections.narration: on the weaker side: calorie density is very high because that makes portion control a real issue. protein is useful but not elite because nuts help, but they are not protein stars. cost or allergy issues can limit use because practical downsides still count.
